description: Essential phrases for buses, trains, trams, and taxis in Finland

items.notes: 'Linja-autoasema' is a compound from 'linja-auto' (bus, literally 'line car') + 'asema' (station). Major cities have central bus stations ('linja-autoasema'). In Helsinki, the main bus station is at Kamppi.

items.english: What time does the bus leave?
items.notes: 'Mihin aikaan' means 'at what time'. 'Bussi' is the informal word for 'linja-auto' (bus). 'Lähtee' is from 'lähteä' (to leave/depart). Finnish public transport is known for punctuality.
'Lippu' means 'ticket'. 'Maksaa' means 'to cost'. Ticket prices in Finland vary by zone and operator. In Helsinki, the HSL travel card ('matkakortti') offers discounted fares.
'Menolipun' is the genitive/accusative of 'menolippu' (one-way ticket), a compound from 'meno' (going) + 'lippu' (ticket). One-way tickets are standard for city transport.

'Menopaluulipun' is the genitive/accusative of 'menopaluulippu' (return ticket), a compound from 'meno' (going) + 'paluu' (return) + 'lippu' (ticket). Often cheaper than two single tickets for long-distance travel.

'Paikka' means 'seat/place'. 'Vapaa' means 'free/available'. Finns generally respect personal space on public transport. It's polite to ask before sitting next to someone on a less crowded bus or train.
'Seuraava' means 'next'. 'Pysäkki' means 'stop' (bus/tram stop). Finnish buses and trams typically announce stops automatically. Press the stop button ('STOP') before your stop.

items.english: Where do I need to transfer?
items.notes: 'Minun pitää' means 'I need to/must'. 'Vaihtaa' means 'to change/transfer'. Transfers between buses, trams, and metro in Helsinki are included in a single ticket within the time limit.

items.english: How long does the journey take?
items.notes: 'Kuinka kauan' means 'how long'. 'Matka' means 'journey/trip'. 'Kestää' means 'to last/take time'. Useful for planning travel, especially for longer intercity routes.
'Juna' means 'train'. 'Menee' is from 'mennä' (to go). 'Helsinkiin' is the illative case of 'Helsinki' (to Helsinki). Finnish city names take case endings: Tampereelle, Turkuun, Ouluun.
'Metroasema' is a compound from 'metro' + 'asema' (station). Helsinki is the only Finnish city with a metro system. The metro runs from east Helsinki through the center to Espoo.

'Raitiovaunulipun' is the genitive/accusative of 'raitiovaunulippu' (tram ticket). 'Raitiovaunu' means 'tram' (literally 'rail wagon'). The colloquial word is 'ratikka'. Helsinki and Tampere have tram networks.

items.english: When does the next train leave?
items.notes: 'Milloin' means 'when'. 'Seuraava' means 'next'. VR (Valtion Rautatiet) operates Finnish train services. Check schedules at vr.fi or the VR app.
'Myöhässä' is the inessive case of 'myöhä' (late), meaning 'delayed/running late'. Finnish public transport is generally very punctual, but winter weather can cause delays.

items.english: Can I buy a ticket on the bus?
items.notes: 'Bussista' is the elative case of 'bussi' (from the bus). In many Finnish cities, you can pay on the bus with a contactless card, but it's usually more expensive than a pre-purchased ticket or travel card.

'Taksitolppa' is a compound from 'taksi' (taxi) + 'tolppa' (post/stand). Taxi stands are marked with a yellow 'TAKSI' sign. Finnish taxis are safe but relatively expensive. The meter starts when you get in.

items.english: How do I get to the center?
items.notes: 'Pääsen' is the first person of 'päästä' (to get to/reach). 'Keskustaan' is the illative of 'keskusta' (center). 'Keskusta' refers to the city center/downtown area in Finnish cities.

'Aikataulun' is the genitive/accusative of 'aikataulu' (timetable), a compound from 'aika' (time) + 'taulu' (board/table). Digital timetables are available on apps like HSL (Helsinki) and the Matkahuolto app for long-distance buses.
'Numero' means 'number'. 'Viisi' means 'five'. Replace the number as needed. Finnish bus numbers are displayed on the front and side of the bus. Major routes in cities typically have low numbers.
'Mihin' means 'where to' (illative of 'mikä'). 'Menee' is from 'mennä' (to go). Ask the driver or check the destination displayed on the front of the bus.
